Name: profiling_guided_kernel_fusion
Title: Profiling-Guided Kernel Fusion for Memory-Bandwidth-Optimized Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Transformer inference on commodity GPUs is primarily memory-bandwidth-bound, and profiling-guided kernel fusion can significantly reduce memory round-trips and improve tensor-core occupancy, leading to measurable wall-clock latency improvements.
Related Work: Recent work has explored collaborative inference (SplitLLM), PIM acceleration (PAISE), FPGA optimizations (CHOSEN, Enhancing Transformer Inference Efficiency on FPGA), and quantization (Quantix). However, these approaches either focus on high-level architectural changes or hardware-specific optimizations. Our proposal distinguishes itself by targeting kernel-level throughput optimization through profiling-guided fusion, directly addressing the memory-bandwidth bottleneck on commodity GPUs. This is not a trivial extension, as it requires detailed profiling and CUDA/Triton kernel engineering to achieve measurable speedups.
Abstract: Transformer inference on commodity GPUs is increasingly memory-bandwidth-bound, limiting throughput and scalability. We propose profiling-guided kernel fusion, a systematic approach to optimize memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy through CUDA/Triton kernel engineering. By profiling transformer inference workloads, we identify critical memory-bound operations and fuse them into optimized kernels that minimize memory round-trips and maximize tensor-core utilization. Our approach integrates a lightweight profiler to measure achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity, guiding kernel fusion decisions. We demonstrate measurable wall-clock latency improvements on single-GPU setups for transformer models, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token. Our contributions include a reproducible benchmark harness, profiled kernel traces, and end-to-end speedups that practitioners can deploy immediately.
Experiments: Profile transformer inference workloads (e.g., GPT-2, Llama2-7B) on a commodity GPU to identify memory-bound operations and bottlenecks.
Fuse identified operations into optimized CUDA/Triton kernels, minimizing memory round-trips and maximizing tensor-core occupancy.
Measure achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity before and after kernel fusion.
Compare wall-clock latency (p50/p99), throughput under batching, and energy per token against baseline implementations.
Evaluate the impact of kernel fusion on end-to-end inference speedups across different batch sizes and sequence lengths.
Report variance across runs as measured jitter, ensuring reproducibility with pinned driver/CUDA versions and archived trace files.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Kernel fusion may introduce additional complexity in kernel implementation, requiring careful engineering to avoid performance regressions.
The approach may be limited by the specific architecture of the GPU, and optimizations may not generalize across different hardware platforms.
Profiling overhead could impact measured speedups, requiring careful calibration of the profiler to minimize its impact.
The approach may not yield significant improvements for compute-bound workloads, limiting its applicability to memory-bandwidth-bound scenarios.
